title: The case for libraries that don't lend
summary: Argue whether reference libraries — collections you visit but cannot borrow from — still have a place in an age of digital access.
topic: The case for libraries that don't lend
prompt: Write an argumentative essay (350–500 words) exploring whether reference libraries — institutions where you read on-site but cannot take books home — still serve a meaningful purpose in the digital age, or whether they are an expensive relic. Use a cleft sentence, a concession ('Granted, …' or 'Admittedly, …'), at least one passive construction, and a participle clause. Include at least one concrete example (a specific reference library, a country, or a research context). Take a clear position.
hintStarters: What a reference library offers that no search engine can replicate is…
Admittedly, scanning a document at home and consulting it in a reading room produce broadly similar…
Stripped of lending rights, the library becomes something more deliberate…

hintVocab.meaningVi: thư viện tham khảo (chỉ đọc tại chỗ)
một tổ chức đang có nguy cơ biến mất
khám phá tình cờ may mắn
làm việc trực tiếp với tài liệu gốc
bộ sưu tập được chọn lọc kỹ lưỡng
cơ sở dữ liệu độc quyền
bị kiểm soát / giới hạn quyền truy cập
ký ức / kiến thức tích lũy của một tổ chức
nơi ẩn dật để nghiên cứu học thuật
dân chủ hóa quyền tiếp cận

sampleText:
A library that refuses to let you take its books home sounds, at first, like a library that has forgotten its purpose. Yet the reference library — the kind where you order a volume at a desk, sit in a high-ceilinged room, and return everything before you leave — is not a failure of generosity. It is a different kind of institution, built around a different idea of what knowledge requires.

The most obvious argument against reference-only collections is that digitisation has made them redundant. Vast archives are now available from a laptop, and the British Library's digitised newspaper collection, for example, lets a researcher in Hanoi consult a Victorian periodical without booking a reading room. Admittedly, this is a genuine gain, and no honest defender of physical collections should pretend otherwise. Scanning has extended access to material that was once the preserve of those wealthy or mobile enough to travel to London.

Even so, what digitisation cannot reproduce is the experience of working with a document as an object. A researcher handling a sixteenth-century map at the Biblioteca Nazionale in Florence encounters details — marginal annotations, ink colour, the weight of the parchment — that no high-resolution image fully captures. Being limited to on-site use also means the material is preserved: borrowing spreads deterioration, and rare items are routinely damaged by well-meaning readers who take them home.

Beyond conservation, the reference library performs a social function that is harder to quantify. It is a space designed to concentrate the mind. There are no deliveries to answer, no kitchen to wander into. The institutional architecture of the reading room — the silence enforced by shared presence, the slight formality of requesting a book — nudges readers toward the kind of sustained, slow attention that produces original thought. Digital libraries are extraordinarily convenient, but convenience is not always what scholarship needs.

Granted, maintaining such spaces is expensive, and the argument that we should fund elaborate buildings so that researchers can think more clearly will not survive a budget meeting without stronger evidence. The case must rest not on nostalgia but on what these institutions concretely produce: doctoral dissertations that changed a field, historical discoveries made only because a librarian knew where a misfiled letter sat, connections made between volumes shelved next to each other by deliberate design.

The reference library does not lend books because it is trying to withhold them. It does not lend them because some things are too valuable, in every sense, to be sent out into the world without a guarantee of return.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Cleft sentence to foreground an argument's core claim
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What digitisation cannot reproduce is the experience of working with a document as an object' dùng cấu trúc 'What … is …' để đặt luận điểm quan trọng nhất ở vị trí nhấn mạnh. Thay vì nói 'Digitisation cannot reproduce the experience …', câu chẻ đôi (cleft) giúp đặt 'the experience' ở phần kết câu — nơi có trọng lượng nhất về mặt diễn đạt.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What digitisation cannot reproduce is the experience of working with a document as an object' uses a what-cleft to place the essay's pivotal claim in the most emphatic position. Rather than stating 'Digitisation cannot reproduce the experience …', the cleft moves 'the experience' to the end of the sentence, where it carries the most rhetorical weight.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Participle clause as subject to compress a complex idea
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Being limited to on-site use also means the material is preserved' dùng mệnh đề phân từ bị động ('Being limited …') làm chủ ngữ. Cách này nén lại một điều kiện phức tạp ('the fact that readers are limited to on-site use') thành một cụm phân từ gọn nhẹ, giúp câu văn đi thẳng đến hệ quả ('the material is preserved') mà không cần mệnh đề chính phụ dài dòng.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Being limited to on-site use also means the material is preserved' uses a passive participle clause as the grammatical subject. This condenses the complex condition ('the fact that readers are limited to on-site use') into a compact phrase, allowing the sentence to move directly to its consequence without a lengthy subordinate clause.

sampleTranslationVi:
Một thư viện từ chối cho bạn mang sách về nghe có vẻ, thoạt đầu, như là một thư viện đã quên mất mục đích của mình. Tuy nhiên, thư viện tham khảo — loại nơi bạn đặt yêu cầu một cuốn sách tại quầy, ngồi trong phòng đọc có trần cao, và trả lại mọi thứ trước khi rời đi — không phải là sự thất bại của lòng hào phóng. Đó là một loại tổ chức khác, được xây dựng xung quanh một ý tưởng khác về những gì kiến thức cần đến.

Lý lẽ rõ ràng nhất chống lại các bộ sưu tập chỉ để tham khảo là số hóa đã làm cho chúng trở nên lỗi thời. Các kho lưu trữ rộng lớn hiện có thể truy cập từ máy tính xách tay, và bộ sưu tập báo được số hóa của Thư viện Anh, chẳng hạn, cho phép một nhà nghiên cứu ở Hà Nội tham khảo một tạp chí thời Victoria mà không cần đặt phòng đọc. Thừa nhận rằng, đây là một lợi ích thực sự, và không có người bảo vệ trung thực nào của các bộ sưu tập vật lý nên giả vờ khác đi. Việc số hóa đã mở rộng quyền truy cập vào tài liệu từng là đặc quyền của những người đủ giàu có hoặc có điều kiện di chuyển đến London.

Dẫu vậy, điều mà số hóa không thể tái tạo là trải nghiệm làm việc với tài liệu như một vật thể. Một nhà nghiên cứu cầm trên tay một tấm bản đồ thế kỷ XVI tại Thư viện Quốc gia ở Florence gặp những chi tiết — các ghi chú bên lề, màu mực, trọng lượng của tờ giấy da — mà không có hình ảnh độ phân giải cao nào có thể nắm bắt đầy đủ. Việc giới hạn chỉ được sử dụng tại chỗ cũng có nghĩa là tài liệu được bảo tồn: việc cho mượn làm lan rộng sự xuống cấp, và các vật phẩm quý hiếm thường xuyên bị hư hỏng bởi những người đọc có thiện chí mang chúng về nhà.

Ngoài việc bảo tồn, thư viện tham khảo còn thực hiện một chức năng xã hội khó định lượng hơn. Đó là một không gian được thiết kế để tập trung tư duy. Không có giao hàng cần trả lời, không có nhà bếp để lang thang vào. Kiến trúc thể chế của phòng đọc — sự im lặng được duy trì bởi sự hiện diện chung, sự trang trọng nhẹ nhàng của việc yêu cầu một cuốn sách — thúc đẩy người đọc hướng đến loại chú ý bền vững, chậm rãi tạo ra tư duy độc đáo. Thư viện số vô cùng tiện lợi, nhưng sự tiện lợi không phải lúc nào cũng là điều mà học thuật cần.

Thừa nhận rằng, việc duy trì những không gian như vậy tốn kém, và lý lẽ rằng chúng ta nên tài trợ cho những tòa nhà công phu để các nhà nghiên cứu có thể suy nghĩ rõ ràng hơn sẽ không tồn tại được trong một cuộc họp ngân sách nếu thiếu bằng chứng mạnh mẽ hơn. Lý lẽ phải dựa không phải vào sự hoài niệm mà vào những gì các tổ chức này tạo ra một cách cụ thể: các luận án tiến sĩ đã thay đổi một lĩnh vực, những khám phá lịch sử chỉ được thực hiện vì một thủ thư biết một bức thư bị lưu nhầm chỗ nằm ở đâu, những mối liên hệ được tạo ra giữa các cuốn sách được xếp bên cạnh nhau theo thiết kế có chủ ý.

Thư viện tham khảo không cho mượn sách vì nó đang cố gắng giữ chặt chúng. Nó không cho mượn vì một số thứ quá quý giá, theo mọi nghĩa, để được gửi ra thế giới mà không có sự đảm bảo sẽ trở lại.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses a 'what' cleft to foreground the essay's central contrast?
options: Digitisation is what cannot reproduce the experience of working with a document as an object.
What digitisation cannot reproduce is the experience of working with a document as an object.
The experience what digitisation cannot is to reproduce as an object.
What is the experience that digitisation cannot working with a document object reproduce.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'What [subject + verb] is [noun phrase]' is the standard cleft structure; it places the key idea at the end for emphasis.
mcQuestions.hint: The cleft structure is 'What … is …', with the important information after 'is'.

options: The process of converting physical information into digital format
The design of new digital devices
The science of storing data in printed form
The policy of restricting internet access
mcQuestions.explanation: Digitisation refers to converting analogue content — books, photographs, maps — into digital files.
mcQuestions.hint: Think about what happens when libraries photograph or scan their collections.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'admittedly' correctly as a concession?
options: Admittedly, this is a genuine gain, and no honest defender of physical collections should pretend otherwise.
Admittedly this gain genuine is a and no honest pretend should defender otherwise physical.
This is a genuine gain, admittedly should pretend no honest defender collections physical otherwise.
No defender admittedly pretend honest should this is a genuine gain otherwise physical.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Admittedly,' followed by a complete clause acknowledges a point that partly supports the opposing view — the classic concession structure.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the option where 'Admittedly,' is followed by a grammatically complete sentence.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which passive construction correctly describes what happens to rare items?
options: Well-meaning readers routinely damage rare items when they take them home.
Rare items are routinely damaged by well-meaning readers who take them home.
Rare items routinely damage well-meaning readers who taking them home.
Taking home rare items routinely damaged well-meaning readers.
mcQuestions.explanation: The passive 'are routinely damaged' puts 'rare items' in subject position, foregrounding the things at risk rather than the people causing the damage.
mcQuestions.hint: Which sentence makes 'rare items' the grammatical subject?

options: A discovery made by following a strict research plan
A discovery made by chance while looking for something else
A discovery published in a peer-reviewed journal
A discovery that turns out to be incorrect
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Serendipitous' describes a fortunate accident — finding something valuable without specifically searching for it.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'serendipity' — the gift of making pleasant discoveries by chance.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase best describes a 'reading room'?
options: A bedroom with many bookshelves
A dedicated, often formal space inside a library where visitors read on-site
A digital room you enter through a website
A classroom used only for literacy lessons
mcQuestions.explanation: A reading room is the formal, supervised space in a reference library where patrons consult materials they cannot take away.
mcQuestions.hint: It is a physical room, not a digital one, and it is associated with reference libraries.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to be superseded by' mean?
options: To be supported and funded by
To be replaced by something newer or more effective
To be supervised closely by
To be legally protected by
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Supersede' means to take the place of something; if technology supersedes a method, it replaces it.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'super-' meaning above or over — something that comes above and pushes the old thing out.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a participle clause as subject?
options: Being limited to on-site use also means the material is preserved.
The material is preserved being on-site limited to use also means.
Also means being limited preserved to on-site the material use.
Limited on-site use also the material preserved being means.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Being limited to on-site use' is a passive participle clause functioning as the grammatical subject of 'means'.
mcQuestions.hint: The subject of the sentence should be an -ing phrase, not a noun.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to concentrate the mind' mean?
options: To place a mind in a concentrated solution
To help someone focus their thinking on a task
To reduce a person's intelligence
To memorise a large amount of information
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Concentrate the mind' is an idiom meaning to sharpen one's focus and remove distractions.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'concentrate' as 'focus' — what does focusing your mind help you do?

options: A shared public space where knowledge is accessible to everyone
The average level of intelligence in a population
A park where academics gather to discuss ideas
The common errors found in academic writing
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Commons' refers to shared resources held by a community; an 'intellectual commons' is a shared space or collection for intellectual life.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of the 'commons' as something shared by everyone — what kind of shared resource is being described?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best pivots from a concession back to the main argument?
options: For example,
In other words,
Even so, what digitisation cannot reproduce is the experience of working with a document as an object.
Similarly,
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so' acknowledges the concession just made and reasserts the main argument — the classic concession-rebuttal pattern.
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrase signals that despite the admitted point, the opposite conclusion still holds?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence maintains the most formal academic register?
options: You can't really argue with digital access being pretty great for most people.
On-site consultation of archival material preserves both the item and the integrity of the researcher's encounter with it.
Libraries are cool but sometimes you just want to take the book home, right?
Basically the whole point of reference libraries is that they're kind of old-fashioned.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'On-site consultation', 'archival material', and 'integrity of the researcher's encounter' are precise, formal collocations appropriate to academic argument.
mcQuestions.hint: Avoid contractions, informal hedges, and sentence-final question tags.

options: Newly published academic textbooks
Historical documents and records preserved for research purposes
Digital files stored in a cloud system
Popular fiction kept in a public library
mcQuestions.explanation: Archival material comprises documents, letters, photographs, and other records preserved because of their historical or research value.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of an 'archive' — what does it hold and why?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses a relative clause to add essential information?
options: A researcher handling a sixteenth-century map at the Biblioteca Nazionale in Florence encounters details that no high-resolution image fully captures.
A researcher at the Biblioteca Nazionale in Florence a sixteenth-century map handling encounters details that fully captures.
Details that no high-resolution image at the Biblioteca Nazionale encounters a researcher handling fully.
Handling a researcher encounters a sixteenth-century map that details the Biblioteca Nazionale fully.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'that no high-resolution image fully captures' is a defining relative clause specifying which details are meant.
mcQuestions.hint: The relative clause begins with 'that' and immediately follows the noun it modifies.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to foster deep reading' mean?
options: To encourage sustained, careful, and thoughtful engagement with a text
To scan text quickly for key facts
To read aloud to children before bed
To translate a text into another language
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Foster' means to encourage or promote; 'deep reading' refers to slow, concentrated reading that allows genuine comprehension and reflection.
mcQuestions.hint: What is the opposite of skimming — and why might a library environment encourage it?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best concludes an argument by reframing its central term?
options: In conclusion, reference libraries have both advantages and disadvantages.

The reference library does not lend books because it is trying to withhold them. It does not lend them because some things are too valuable, in every sense, to be sent out into the world without a guarantee of return.
To sum up, digital access is better for most readers and reference libraries are mostly outdated.
Both sides have valid points and we should support all types of libraries equally.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B reframes the essay's central apparent paradox — a library that won't lend — as a form of protection rather than restriction, landing on a clear and memorable position.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option ends on a precise, original observation rather than a vague summary?

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of a piece of knowledge — a document, a collection, or an idea — that you think should require physical presence to access. What is it about the physical encounter that matters, and what does your answer reveal about the relationship between convenience and understanding?
